Collection › Tang Dynasty › ro-tang-001 — Sancai Jia Lan Amphora

Reference Object ro-tang-001

Tang Dynasty Sancai Jia Lan Amphora with Dragon Handles and Court Ladies

唐三彩加藍 — Tang Dynasty, 618–907 CE — Probably Gongyi (Huangye Kiln), Henan Province

Tang Dynasty Sancai Jia Lan 三彩加藍 Cobalt Underglaze Lead Glaze Dragon Handles Court Ladies Silk Road Cobalt DEE · SDE · LIE · PLIC GOM Examined

Object Description

A large buff earthenware amphora combining three distinct Tang ceramic traditions: the amphora form ultimately derived from Central Asian and Hellenistic metalwork via Silk Road transmission; the polychrome sancai glaze system characteristic of Tang funerary and ceremonial ware; and the rare cobalt underglaze technique (jia lan, 加藍) associated with imported West Asian cobalt reaching China through Silk Road trade during the High Tang period.

The vessel carries a tall cylindrical neck with a flared cup-shaped mouth, two sculptural S-shaped dragon handles surmounting the shoulder, and four applied high-relief court ladies in polychrome sancai costumes distributed around the body. The cobalt-blue ground is produced by a cobalt-bearing slip applied beneath a transparent lead glaze. The polychrome sancai decoration in amber, green, and cream-white is applied over this base system. The unglazed buff foot ring exposes the characteristic porous earthenware body of Tang sancai production.

Sancai Jia Lan (三彩加藍) — Rare Technology: Unlike ordinary Tang sancai, where amber, green, and cream glazes are applied directly to the body, sancai jia lan introduces a cobalt-bearing layer beneath a transparent lead glaze. This innovation produces unusually deep optical effects and reflects the use of imported cobalt associated with Silk Road trade — one of the earliest applications of cobalt in Chinese ceramic history. Large dragon-handled amphorae in this technique are considerably rarer than ordinary funerary sancai vessels.

Sancai Jia Lan Manufacturing Technology

The sancai jia lan technique represents one of the most technologically sophisticated Tang glaze systems, involving a multi-layer sequential application process fired at low temperature (<1000°C) in an oxidising atmosphere.

Manufacturing Sequence — Sancai Jia Lan System

1

Body Forming

Buff earthenware body wheel-thrown. Applied court ladies moulded separately and attached.

2

White Slip

White preparation layer applied over the body to create a clean ground for the cobalt and lead glazes.

3

Cobalt Slip

Cobalt-bearing slip (imported West Asian cobalt) applied over the white layer to defined areas.

4

Lead Glaze

Transparent PbO-rich lead glaze applied over the entire surface including the cobalt areas.

5

Sancai Glazes

Polychrome amber, green, and cream-white sancai glazes applied to figurative elements.

6

Firing

Single low-temperature firing (<1000°C) in oxidising atmosphere. All layers mature simultaneously.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis — Dronova Method

The amphora was examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) applying the Dronova Method (DEE–SDE–LIE–PLIC) — a systematic four-category optical authentication protocol. The intact glaze surface acts as a transparent optical window allowing direct access to internal microstructural features without destructive sampling. Observations were compared with published analytical studies of authenticated Tang sancai (XRF, Raman spectroscopy, INAA, lead-isotope analysis).

DEE

Depth-Enhanced Effect — Cobalt Optical Depth

The cobalt ground exhibits pronounced internal optical depth rather than a flat surface colour. Reflected and oblique illumination reveal the transparent lead glaze, suspended gas microbubbles, and the deep cobalt layer below. Light travels through multiple transparent layers before reaching the observer, producing genuine three-dimensional spatial depth. Modern reproductions placing cobalt directly into the glaze produce a visually flat surface lacking DEE.

SDE

Surface Diffusion Effect — Cobalt Halos

The boundary between the cobalt field and cream-coloured appliqué figures displays gradual diffusion halos produced by cobalt migration during firing. Soft blue transition zones, asymmetric diffusion gradients, and microbubble continuity across boundaries are visible. Artificial repainting cannot reproduce this continuous ionic diffusion profile.

LIE

Layer Interface Effect — Glaze Stratigraphy

Oblique illumination reveals glaze stratigraphy through the intact surface: interface between cobalt slip and lead glaze, overlap between dragon-handle glaze and body glaze, glaze pooling, and sequential coating structure. No polishing or cross-section preparation is required — the intact glaze itself acts as an optical window.

PLIC

Patina–Lead Interface Crackle — Natural Ageing

An exceptionally uniform fine crackle covers the entire glazed surface. The crack network shows natural sediment consolidation, continuous crack morphology across all glaze colours (blue, amber, green, white), and identical ageing in every glaze zone. Sediment penetrates deeply with centuries of mineral consolidation — artificial staining produces only superficial deposits.

GOM Documentation — DEE / SDE: Cobalt Field and Diffusion

DEE / SDE

Fig. 4. DEE / SDE — Cobalt field with PLIC crackle network and soft diffusion halo at boundary with cream-white appliqué figure. The cobalt diffusion produces a gradual blue transition zone rather than a sharp painted edge, confirming authentic in-firing ionic migration. Lead-glass microbubbles visible in the cobalt field.

LIE

Fig. 5. LIE — Layer interface at dragon handle junction. The cobalt/lead glaze interface and sancai overglaze boundaries are visible through the intact surface under oblique illumination. Sequential coating structure confirmed. Melt-flow structures at colour boundaries indicate simultaneous low-temperature firing.

GOM Documentation — PLIC: Crackle and Natural Ageing

PLIC

Fig. 6. PLIC — Fine crackle network across the cobalt lead glaze. The interconnected crack network carries natural sediment consolidation with deep mineral penetration. Identical crack morphology and ageing pattern are consistent across all glaze colours (cobalt, amber, green, white), confirming unified long-term natural ageing.

1. DEE — Cobalt Optical Depth

The deep cobalt field exhibits genuine three-dimensional optical depth rather than a flat pigmented surface. Under GOM, the transparent lead glaze above the cobalt slip is clearly distinguishable as a separate optical medium through which the cobalt layer below can be observed. Gas microbubbles suspended within the lead glaze contribute to internal light scattering, enhancing the DEE phenomenon. This stratified optical system — body → cobalt slip → lead glaze → observer — is the direct optical manifestation of the sancai jia lan manufacturing sequence and cannot be reproduced by glaze systems in which cobalt is mixed directly into the lead glaze.

2. SDE — Cobalt Diffusion Halos

At the boundaries between the cobalt blue ground and the cream-white slip of the applied court ladies, GOM reveals soft blue diffusion halos produced by cobalt ionic migration during low-temperature firing. The asymmetric gradients, continuous microbubble distribution across the boundary, and melt-flow structures confirm that the cobalt and cream areas matured simultaneously in a single firing event. Post-firing repainting or artificial surface application would produce sharp mechanical boundaries rather than the observed diffusion profiles.

3. LIE — Layer Interface Visibility

Oblique illumination through the intact transparent lead glaze makes the internal layer stratigraphy optically accessible without destructive preparation. The interface between the cobalt slip layer and the overlying lead glaze is distinguishable by differential light refraction. At the dragon handle junctions, the overlap sequence between handle glaze and body glaze is visible, confirming that handles were applied before the final glaze application. Sequential glaze pooling and coating structures provide direct visual evidence of the original manufacturing sequence.

4. PLIC — Ancient Crackle and Sediment Consolidation

The entire glazed surface carries an exceptionally uniform fine crackle characteristic of Tang lead-glazed earthenware. The crack network extends continuously and with identical morphology across all glaze colour zones — cobalt blue, amber, green, and cream-white — confirming that all areas aged simultaneously within the same environmental conditions. Sediment penetration into the crack channels shows deep mineral consolidation consistent with centuries of natural ageing. Artificial staining characteristically produces only superficial deposits without continuous internal filling.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The four optical diagnostic systems evaluated by the Dronova Method form a coherent and mutually reinforcing authentication profile. The DEE confirms the stratified cobalt/lead glaze architecture; the SDE demonstrates authentic in-firing cobalt diffusion; the LIE reveals the original manufacturing sequence through the intact surface; and the PLIC documents centuries of unified natural ageing. The combination creates a distinctive optical fingerprint evaluable entirely through non-destructive optical examination.

Diagnostic SystemObservationAuthentication Value
DEE — Cobalt optical depthStratified glaze architecture confirmed; genuine 3D spatial depthVery High
SDE — Cobalt diffusion halosIonic diffusion gradients at cobalt/cream boundaries; melt-flow structuresVery High
LIE — Layer interfacesSequential coating structure visible; handle/body overlap confirmedHigh
PLIC — Ancient crackleUniform fine crackle with deep sediment consolidation across all glaze coloursVery High

Key Diagnostic Markers

DEE: Stratified cobalt/lead glaze optical depth — genuine 3-dimensional spatial depth
DEE: Lead-glass microbubble population within the transparent lead glaze
SDE: Soft cobalt diffusion halos at cobalt/cream boundaries
SDE: Asymmetric diffusion gradients — in-firing ionic migration confirmed
LIE: Cobalt slip / lead glaze interface visible through intact surface
LIE: Sequential coating structure and handle/body overlap confirmed
PLIC: Uniform fine crackle continuous across all glaze colour zones
PLIC: Deep sediment consolidation in crack channels — centuries of natural ageing
Buff porous Tang earthenware body with concentric wheel-throwing marks
Natural glaze termination above unglazed foot ring
No restoration detected

Diagnostic Confidence: Very High — Fully consistent with authentic Tang sancai jia lan

References

Published XRF, Raman spectroscopy, INAA, and lead-isotope analyses of authenticated Tang sancai from Gongyi/Huangye kilns, confirming PbO-rich lead-glaze chemistry and West Asian cobalt chromophore.

Shaanxi History Museum, Xi’an — Tang sancai jia lan comparanda; published catalogue.

National Museum of China, Beijing — Dragon-handled amphora comparanda in Tang sancai.

Published archaeometric studies of Silk Road cobalt in Tang ceramics (LA-ICP-MS, lead-isotope analysis) linking cobalt chromophores to West Asian sources.
